title: Acute diarrhea after eating out

chart.oneLiner: 30M presents with 2 days of frequent diarrhea (now with some blood), crampy abdominal pain, fever, and nausea after a barbecue.

opening: I've had bad diarrhea and stomach cramps since the weekend — there's been a bit of blood the last day.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 30-year-old man, uncomfortable, dehydrated. Good historian.

HPI: 2 days of frequent loose stools (8–10x/day), now with streaks of blood and mucus, crampy abdominal pain, fever, and nausea. Ate undercooked chicken at a barbecue ~2 days before onset (only if asked about food/exposures). A couple of other attendees got sick too (only if asked about sick contacts). NO recent antibiotics, NO recent travel abroad, NO immunosuppression.
PMH: none. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: healthy, no recent travel.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable, dehydrated (dry mucous membranes).
- Vitals: BP 116/72, HR 100, RR 16, 38.4°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: diffuse mild tenderness, active/hyperactive bowel sounds, no peritonism.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the undercooked chicken or sick contacts unless asked about food/exposures or sick contacts.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.StoolStudies: Stool culture and a multiplex pathogen panel sent; fecal leukocytes present (inflammatory diarrhea). (Culture later: Campylobacter.)
labs.CBC: WBC 13.0 with left shift, Hgb 14.5, Plt 250.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 3.5, BUN 22, Cr 1.1 — mild dehydration.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Deep symmetric or biphasic T-wave inversions in V2-V3 with preserved R waves; pain-free.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Deep symmetric or biphasic T-wave inversions in V2-V3 with preserved R waves; pain-free. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

searchTerms: biphasic T wave V2 V3 Wellens

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Left lower lobe volume loss with a linear opacity and slight mediastinal shift toward the opacity.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Left lower lobe volume loss with a linear opacity and slight mediastinal shift toward the opacity. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing atelectasis volume loss

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Bacterial (inflammatory) gastroenteritis — e.g., Campylobacter/Salmonella/Shigella (leading,7). Shiga-toxin E. coli (CANT-MISS — affects management,4). Viral gastroenteritis (core,3). C. difficile (core,2). Inflammatory bowel disease (core,3). Appendicitis (CANT-MISS to consider,2). Names an infectious inflammatory diarrhea with food exposure (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: bacterial (inflammatory) gastroenteritis — e.g., Campylobacter/Salmonella/Shigella. Must-not-miss: Shiga-toxin E. coli (STEC) — affects management; appendicitis. Also reasonable: viral gastroenteritis, C. difficile, inflammatory bowel disease. Names an infectious inflammatory diarrhea with food exposure.

coreActions.item: Names an infectious inflammatory diarrhea with food exposure

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Stool studies — culture and/or pathogen panel (CANT-MISS,5). CBC (2). BMP for hydration/electrolytes (3). C. difficile toxin if risk factors (2). Blood cultures if febrile/toxic (2). Consider ova & parasites only if indicated (1). PENALTY: empiric antibiotics before considering Shiga-toxin E. coli (-2 — addressed in management).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: stool studies — culture and/or pathogen panel; CBC; BMP for hydration/electrolytes; C. difficile toxin if risk factors; blood cultures given fever; ova & parasites only if indicated.

criticalActions.item: Stool studies — culture and/or pathogen panel

coreActions.item: C. difficile toxin if risk factors

penalties.item: Empiric antibiotics before considering Shiga-toxin E. coli (addressed in management)

requiredTests: Stool studies — culture and/or pathogen panel

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = Wellens pattern: deep symmetric or biphasic T-wave inversions in V2-V3 with preserved R waves; patient currently pain-free. CREDIT: identifies biphasic/deep T inversions in V2-V3 (7); recognizes the critical proximal-LAD stenosis warning pattern (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: dismissing the changes as normal/benign because the patient is pain-free (-3).
keyFindings: biphasic / deep T inversions in V2-V3
recognizes the proximal LAD warning pattern
criticalFindings: Wellens syndrome (critical LAD stenosis warning pattern).
commonMistakes: dismissing the T-wave changes because the patient is currently pain-free
steps.idealAnswer: Wellens pattern: biphasic or deeply inverted T waves in V2-V3 — a warning for critical proximal LAD stenosis.

criticalActions: Wellens syndrome (critical LAD stenosis warning pattern).
coreActions: biphasic / deep T inversions in V2-V3
recognizes the proximal LAD warning pattern

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = atelectasis: left lower lobe volume loss with a linear/band opacity and slight ipsilateral mediastinal shift toward the opacity. CREDIT: identifies the linear/band opacity with volume loss (6); distinguishes atelectasis from consolidation/pneumonia (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal or labelling it pneumonia (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Atelectasis: left basal linear opacity with volume loss and ipsilateral mediastinal shift.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Stool shows inflammatory diarrhea with a likely bacterial pathogen after undercooked poultry. What is your diagnosis and one key management caution?
steps.rubric: Bacterial gastroenteritis (e.g., Campylobacter) (6). Notes the caution — avoid empiric antibiotics if Shiga-toxin E. coli is possible (HUS risk), and avoid antimotility agents in bloody/febrile diarrhea (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Bacterial gastroenteritis (likely Campylobacter from undercooked poultry). Key caution: avoid empiric antibiotics if Shiga-toxin E. coli is possible (HUS risk), and avoid antimotility agents in bloody/febrile diarrhea.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH diarrhea: volume and electrolyte repletion (CANT-MISS,7); selective empiric antibiotics for severe/septic/high-risk/bloody (4); AVOID antibiotics if STEC suspected, HUS risk (CANT-MISS,4); antimotility (loperamide) only if no fever/blood (3); stool studies if severe/bloody/high-risk (3). PENALTY: antibiotics in suspected EHEC/STEC (-4); antimotility in febrile bloody diarrhea (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Most acute infectious diarrhea is self-limited: volume and electrolyte repletion (oral or IV). Empiric antibiotics (fluoroquinolone or azithromycin) only for severe disease, sepsis, high-risk hosts, age ≥70, or bloody diarrhea – and AVOID antibiotics if Shiga-toxin E. coli is suspected (HUS risk). Loperamide only if no fever/blood. Stool studies if severe/bloody/high-risk.

criticalActions.item: Avoid antibiotics if STEC suspected (HUS risk)

requiredActions: Avoid antibiotics if STEC suspected (HUS risk)

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 30M presents with 2 days of frequent diarrhea (now with some blood), crampy abdominal pain, fever, and nausea after a barbecue. The diagnosis is Bacterial gastroenteritis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: diffuse mild tenderness, active/hyperactive bowel sounds, no peritonism.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Bacterial gastroenteritis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Bacterial gastroenteritis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
managementPearls: Avoid antibiotics if STEC suspected (HUS risk)
